title: Mildew on Cotton
stainChemistry: Mildew is a living fungal organism that digests organic material in cotton for nutrition. The visible discoloration (gray, black, green) comes from fungal spores and metabolic byproducts. Mildew roots (hyphae) penetrate into the fiber structure, making surface cleaning insufficient. The organism thrives in warm, humid, dark conditions and produces musty volatile organic compounds (odor).
whyThisWorks: An oxidizer (H2O2 or oxygen bleach) kills the mildew organism and breaks down the pigmented metabolic byproducts on cotton. Surfactant removes the dead fungal material. For persistent staining, sunlight exposure provides additional UV oxidation. Preventing recurrence requires addressing the moisture source — mildew returns if conditions remain favorable.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Brush off loose mildew spores outdoors or in ventilated area
2. Apply H2O2 6% to kill remaining organism and bleach discoloration — 10-15 min dwell time
3. Apply NSD to remove dead fungal material — 5 min dwell time
4. Flush with cool water. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
5. Air dry in sunlight if possible — UV provides additional antimicrobial action
warnings: Always test on hidden area first

steps: 1. Brush off loose mildew spores outdoors — wear a mask
2. Mix 1 scoop OxiClean per gallon of warm water. Apply or soak — 30-60 min dwell time
3. Scrub gently with soft brush. Rinse thoroughly
4. Air dry in direct sunlight if possible — UV kills remaining spores
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional for specialized treatment
warnings: Always test cleaning solution on a hidden area first
Air dry until stain is confirmed gone — heat sets most stains

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If mildew stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is mildew on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
